original_hypothesis.title: Bot-Initiated Yield Liquidation Uses Wrong Yield Account, Making Unhealthy Positions Unliquidatable
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In _unstake and _repay, the yield account is resolved via yieldAccounts[msg.sender]. When botAdmin calls these functions to force-liquidate an unhealthy position, the lookup uses yieldAccounts[botAdmin] instead of sd.yieldAccount (the actual user's yield account). Since botAdmin likely has no yield account (address(0)), the require check reverts immediately. Even if botAdmin somehow had a yield account, subsequent operations (deducting yieldShares, requesting withdrawals, claiming) operate on the wrong account, causing underflows or incorrect protocol interactions.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a direct code defect where the wrong address is used for account lookup. The _unstake function resolves vars.yieldAccout = IYieldAccount(yieldAccounts[msg.sender]) at line ~235, then uses it for share accounting at lines ~267-270. When msg.sender is botAdmin (checked at line ~254), this resolves to the bot's account, not the NFT owner's. The same pattern repeats in _repay (~313) and in all protocolRequestWithdrawal/protocolClaimWithdraw implementations which use yieldAccounts[msg.sender].

proof_of_signal.pattern: vars.yieldAccout = IYieldAccount(yieldAccounts[msg.sender]); used when msg.sender == botAdmin; should use sd.yieldAccount

preconditions: A user has an active yield stake position
The position's health factor drops below unstakeHeathFactor
botAdmin is set and attempts to call unstake

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: yieldAccounts[msg.sender] resolves to botAdmin's account instead of NFT owner's
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: accountYieldShares[address(vars.yieldAccout)] -= sd.yieldShare will underflow; protocolRequestWithdrawal withdraws from wrong account
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No fallback to sd.yieldAccount when caller is botAdmin

analysis_questions: Can botAdmin ever successfully force-unstake an unhealthy yield position?
What happens to protocol debt when unhealthy positions cannot be liquidated?
Can users deliberately create positions they know cannot be liquidated?
validation_requirements: Create a yield stake position, let it become unhealthy, attempt bot unstake
Verify the revert at yieldAccounts[botAdmin] == address(0) check
Quantify potential bad debt accumulation from unliquidatable positions

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: User stakes NFT and borrows maximum amount. NFT price drops causing HF < threshold. BotAdmin calls unstake but reverts because yieldAccounts[botAdmin] is address(0). The position becomes permanently unliquidatable, accruing bad debt against the lending pool until the position is so underwater the protocol absorbs the loss.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Unlimited bad debt accumulation - every unhealthy yield position cannot be liquidated by the bot
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All lending pool depositors bear losses from unrecoverable bad debt
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Cannot be fixed without contract upgrade; NFT owner can self-unstake but has no incentive if underwater

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for botAdmin-initiated unstake/repay flow

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: One NFT + borrow amount sufficient for leverage
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable on-chain as failed bot transactions and growing yield debt

validation_result.mechanism: wrong yield account resolution for botAdmin caller
validation_result.impact_summary: Bot-initiated forced unstake and repay of unhealthy yield positions always reverts because yieldAccounts[botAdmin] resolves to address(0), making unhealthy yield positions permanently unliquidatable by the bot.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The _unstake and _repay functions in YieldStakingBase.sol resolve the yield account via yieldAccounts[msg.sender]. When botAdmin calls these functions (which is permitted by the access check at the nftOwner == msg.sender || botAdmin == msg.sender line), the lookup resolves to yieldAccounts[botAdmin] instead of the actual NFT owner's yield account stored in sd.yieldAccount. Since botAdmin typically has no yield account created for itself, yieldAccounts[botAdmin] == address(0), causing an immediate revert at the require(address(vars.yieldAccout) != address(0)) check. Even if botAdmin somehow had a yield account, subsequent operations would operate on the wrong account, causing underflows at accountYieldShares subtraction. The same pattern propagates to all protocolRequestWithdrawal and protocolClaimWithdraw implementations in Lido, Etherfi, and SavingsDai contracts, which all use yieldAccounts[msg.sender] instead of sd.yieldAccount.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly visible in the code. The _unstake function at its entry sets vars.yieldAccout = IYieldAccount(yieldAccounts[msg.sender]), while the access check later allows botAdmin as msg.sender. The sd.yieldAccount field exists and is set correctly during _stake but is never used in _unstake or _repay for account resolution. All three protocol-specific implementations (Lido, Etherfi, SavingsDai) repeat the same yieldAccounts[msg.sender] pattern in protocolRequestWithdrawal and protocolClaimWithdraw.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: When botAdmin calls _unstake, yieldAccounts[msg.sender] resolves to yieldAccounts[botAdmin] which is address(0), causing immediate revert. Should use sd.yieldAccount instead.
Same issue as _unstake - botAdmin caller causes wrong yield account resolution. protocolClaimWithdraw implementations also use yieldAccounts[msg.sender].
Uses yieldAccounts[msg.sender] instead of sd.yieldAccount. When called via _unstake by botAdmin, this resolves to the wrong or zero-address account.
Same wrong account resolution pattern. Claims would fail or operate on wrong account when botAdmin calls _repay.
Same wrong account resolution for SavingsDai. Redeem would attempt from botAdmin's (nonexistent) yield account.

calling_sequence: 1. botAdmin calls unstake(poolId, nft, tokenId, unstakeFine)
5. Even if it didn't revert: accountYieldShares[address(0)] -= sd.yieldShare → underflow revert
6. protocolRequestWithdrawal also uses yieldAccounts[msg.sender] = yieldAccounts[botAdmin] → wrong account
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The entry point is the public unstake() function. When botAdmin calls it, msg.sender is botAdmin. At the start of _unstake, vars.yieldAccout is set to IYieldAccount(yieldAccounts[msg.sender]) which is yieldAccounts[botAdmin]. Since botAdmin never calls createYieldAccount for itself, this mapping returns address(0). The require check on the next line reverts the entire transaction. The correct fix would be to first load the YieldStakeData to get sd.yieldAccount (which was set during the user's original _stake call) and use that address instead of yieldAccounts[msg.sender] when the caller is botAdmin.

deployment_assumptions_checked: botAdmin is set via setBotAdmin by pool admin
config_validation.deployment_evidence: InitConfigYield.s.sol does not call setBotAdmin, but the function exists and is callable by pool admin post-deployment. The bug is a code-level defect regardless of whether botAdmin is configured at deployment time.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is valid: 1) User stakes NFT and borrows maximum amount via yield staking. 2) NFT price drops causing health factor to fall below unstakeHeathFactor threshold. 3) botAdmin (if configured) calls unstake() to force-liquidate the unhealthy position. 4) Transaction reverts at require(address(vars.yieldAccout) != address(0)) because yieldAccounts[botAdmin] is address(0). 5) The position becomes permanently unliquidatable by the bot, accruing bad debt. The NFT owner has no incentive to self-unstake if underwater, leading to systematic bad debt accumulation in the lending pool.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL: Every unhealthy yield staking position cannot be force-liquidated by the bot mechanism. This defeats the entire purpose of the botAdmin liquidation system. In a market downturn, multiple positions could simultaneously become unhealthy, and none could be liquidated, leading to potentially unlimited bad debt accumulation against the lending pool. All lending pool depositors bear the loss from unrecoverable positions. The only mitigation is a contract upgrade, but bad debt accumulated before the upgrade is permanent.
recommended_mitigations: In _unstake: Use sd.yieldAccount (from YieldStakeData) to resolve the yield account instead of yieldAccounts[msg.sender]. When msg.sender is botAdmin, the code should load the stake data first, then use sd.yieldAccount for all yield account operations.
In _repay: Same fix - use sd.yieldAccount instead of yieldAccounts[msg.sender] for the yield account resolution.

In all protocolRequestWithdrawal and protocolClaimWithdraw implementations (Lido, Etherfi, SavingsDai): Accept the yield account address as a parameter from the caller or read it from sd.yieldAccount instead of using yieldAccounts[msg.sender].
Add integration tests that specifically test the botAdmin-initiated unstake and repay flow to prevent regression.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy YieldEthStakingLido (or any yield staking contract) with all dependencies. 2. Configure an NFT with active staking params and set a botAdmin address. 3. Create a yield account for user A and have them stake an NFT with a borrow. 4. Manipulate oracle price to make the position unhealthy (HF < unstakeHeathFactor). 5. Call unstake() from botAdmin address → expect revert with YIELD_ACCOUNT_NOT_EXIST. 6. Verify that the position remains in ACTIVE state and cannot be liquidated by the bot.
uncertainties: If botAdmin also happens to be a user who has created their own yield account, the revert wouldn't happen at the address(0) check but would instead cause incorrect accounting (operating on wrong yield account), which could be even more damaging.
additional_testing_needed: Test what happens if botAdmin coincidentally has a yield account (wrong account accounting)
Test bad debt accumulation over time when positions cannot be liquidated
